mappings.rationaleAI: Paul of Tarsus was the principal architect of early Christian theology, establishing doctrines of justification by faith and salvation extended to Gentiles that transformed Christianity into a universal religion (A.1.a). His epistles shaped Christology (F.1.c), doctrines concerning mankind and salvation (F.4), and profoundly influenced Augustine and Luther (E.5).

Constantine was the first Roman Emperor to profess Christianity, legalizing the faith and granting privileges that led to steady Christianization of the Empire (A.2.a). His building of Constantinople established the eastern center that would shape the growing division between Eastern and Western Christianity.

The Nicene Creed formulated at the Council of Nicaea (325) to counter Arianism is a foundational doctrinal statement (E.4, F.5) affirming the consubstantiality of the Son with the Father (F.1.f). Its creation under Constantine exemplifies the entanglement of theology with imperial politics in the early Church (A.2.b).

The doctrine of the Holy Trinity is a core Christian teaching on the nature of God (F.1.f), formally clarified at Nicaea against Arianism (A.2.b). Augustine's and Aquinas's explorations of Trinitarian theology represent major contributions to post-patristic theological writing (E.5) and Christian philosophy (E.7).

The Pelagian controversy over original sin and divine grace provoked Augustine's influential defense of the necessity of grace, a doctrinal dispute explicitly named in the section outline (A.2.b). The controversy permanently shaped Western Christian doctrines concerning mankind (F.4) and influenced both Catholic and Protestant thought through Augustine's writings (E.5).

Gnosticism flourished in the first through third centuries and was condemned as heretical by Church Fathers including Irenaeus and Tertullian, directly shaping post-apostolic orthodoxy (A.1.b). Its dualistic cosmology dividing material and spiritual realms relates to doctrines concerning the physical world (F.3) and the role of myth in Christianity (E.8).

Mary Magdalene as witness to the crucifixion and first witness to the resurrection is central to New Testament literature (E.1) and the origins of the primitive church (A.1.a). The centuries-long conflation of her identity shaped Christian art (F.7.b), and the Gnostic Gospel of Mary raises questions about myth and authority in early Christianity (E.8).

Charlemagne's fusion of military conquest, forced conversion, and ecclesiastical reform shaped the relation of Christianity to Western culture (A.3.b). Monastic scriptoria preserving texts and advancing education through cathedral schools expanded Christian institutions in the West (A.3.c), while the emphasis on sin and discipline reflects canon law and church polity (F.6.a).

The Lindisfarne Gospels (c. 700 AD) reflect the expansion of Christianity to Anglo-Saxon Britain following Pope Gregory's mission (A.3.c). The lavishly illuminated manuscript blending Celtic, Anglo-Saxon, and Mediterranean traditions represents early medieval Christian art (F.7.a), and its monastic production connects to forms of Christian religious life (F.6.c).

The Byzantine Empire's thousand-year history from 395 AD encompasses the era of imperial Byzantium during which the Eastern Orthodox church developed (B.1). Constantinople's role as a centre of classical and Christian learning shaped the relation of Christianity to Byzantine culture (A.3.b).

The Great Schism of 1054 permanently divided Christianity into Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox communions, directly corresponding to the outline's coverage of the great East-West schism (A.3.d). The filioque dispute, papal authority claims, and liturgical differences underpin distinctions between Eastern and Western Christianity (A.3.a, G.1.c).

The 1122 Concordat of Worms resolved the Investiture Controversy over episcopal appointment, establishing the separation of spiritual and temporal authority (C.1.a). This distinction between ecclesiastical and political power directly concerns the structure of church institutions and canon law (F.6.a).

The twelfth-century transformation included cathedral schools evolving into universities, Gothic architecture, and papal reform reshaping state formation, all relevant to the development of the papacy and religious-cultural characteristics of Latin Christianity in the medieval era (C.1.a, C.1.b).

The Cathar heresy in medieval southern France and the papal campaign against it under Innocent III at the Fourth Lateran Council (1215) reflects the medieval papacy's enforcement of doctrinal conformity (C.1.a). The Cathars' alternative Christian beliefs and their violent suppression concern religious characteristics of Latin Christianity (C.1.b) and normative expressions of belief (F.5).

Marsilius's Defensor Pacis (c. 1324) denied papal temporal authority on scriptural grounds, challenging the development of the papacy as chief spiritual and temporal power (C.1.a). His condemned work later attracted Protestant reformers, forming part of the intellectual background to the Reformation (C.2.a, E.5).

Jan Hus's ideas anticipated the Lutheran Reformation by a century, and his burning at the stake and the subsequent Hussite Wars represent the background of the Protestant Reformation in late medieval Catholicism (C.2.a, D.1.a).

Luther's 1521 appearance before Charles V at the Diet of Worms and his refusal to recant is a defining moment of the German Reformation (D.1.b). The resulting fracture of Western Christendom into Catholic and Protestant confessions directly shaped the Reformation's trajectory (C.2.b).

Marguerite de Navarre was a leading advocate of Catholic Church reform in France who translated early Luther writings into French and sheltered religious reformers (C.2.a, D.1.c). Her activity connects the background of the Reformation in late medieval Catholicism with the early spread of Protestant ideas in France.

Purgatory's theological development from twelfth-century formalization concerns eschatological doctrine (F.1.d) and religious characteristics of Latin Christianity (C.1.b). Its role in the 1054 Great Schism (A.3.d) and renewed divisiveness during the Protestant Reformation through indulgences (C.2) span major divisions in Christian history.

The Thirty Years War (1618-1648) originated in religious conflict between Catholics, Lutherans, and Calvinists, making it a central episode in the wars of religion (D.1.h, C.2.d). The Peace of Westphalia established principles of religious coexistence that reshaped church-state relations across Protestant and Catholic Europe.

The 1549 Book of Common Prayer established unified English-language Protestant liturgy during the English Reformation (D.1.d), becoming central to Anglicanism as a distinct tradition (G.3.d.iii). It functions as a liturgical text shaping worship practice and the church year (F.6.b).

The Covenanters were Scottish Presbyterians who defended their Reformed worship against episcopal imposition, relevant to the Scottish Reformation (D.1.d) and developments in English Protestantism (D.2.d). Their movement shaped Presbyterian church governance and identity (G.3.d.ii).

George Fox founded the Society of Friends (Quakers) in mid-seventeenth-century England, rejecting established Church hierarchy and sacramental worship (G.3.e.iv). Quaker advocacy of religious toleration and equality shaped Protestant dissenting traditions in English Protestantism (D.2.d).

The spread of religious doubt through Enlightenment rationalism, Darwinian evolution, and biblical higher criticism directly challenged Protestant scriptural authority (D.2.c). The trajectory from deism through agnosticism reshaped theology in the modern and post-World War I Protestant eras (D.3, D.4).

John Wesley founded Methodism as a major Protestant movement in eighteenth-century Britain, pioneering evangelical mass preaching to industrial working-class communities (D.2.d, D.3). Methodism as a Free church denomination is explicitly listed in the outline (G.3.d.iv).

The Oxford Movement from 1833 revived ritual, liturgy, and patristic theology within Anglicanism, with Newman arguing the Church of England possessed apostolic succession (G.3.d.iii). Newman's conversion to Roman Catholicism and his theological significance make him central to modern Protestant and Catholic history (D.3, E.5).

Karl Barth was one of the twentieth century's most influential Protestant theologians, whose neo-orthodox rejection of liberal theology reshaped post-World War I Protestantism (D.4). His resistance to Nazi church interference and contribution to the Barmen Declaration connect to Protestant teachings and organization (G.3.b, E.5).

The formal definition of papal infallibility at Vatican I (1870) is a landmark in modern Catholic history (C.4), while the historical elevation of the Bishop of Rome traces the development of the papacy (C.1.a). The doctrine concerns Catholic teachings distinguishing it from other traditions (G.2.c) and normative doctrinal expression (F.5).

Julian of Norwich was a fourteenth-century Christian mystic whose Revelations of Divine Love explored divine love, the nature of sin, and feminine aspects of God (E.6). Her bold vernacular theology addressing God's nature (F.1.a) makes her a significant post-patristic theological writer (E.5).

Augustine's fifth-century theological justification for Christian warfare and Aquinas's thirteenth-century formalization of just war criteria represent major developments in Christian philosophy (E.7) and post-patristic theological writing (E.5).

Alcuin championed Christian doctrine and liberal education within the context of expanding Christianity in the early medieval West (A.3.b relation of Christianity to Western culture 7th-11th century), and his theological writings constitute post-patristic Christian scholarship (E.5).

The episode examines angelic hierarchies in Christian theology, directly covering doctrines concerning intermediary beings such as angels (F.2), and the role of myth and legend in Christianity including angelic representations (E.8).

John Calvin's Reformation in Geneva is directly covered under the Reformation in Switzerland, France, and the Low Countries (D.1.c), the wars of religion (D.1.h), and church institutions and canon law (F.6.a).

The English Reformation triggered by Henry's annulment of his marriage to Catherine is covered under the English Reformation (D.1.d), and the break with Rome relates to the background of the Protestant Reformation (C.2.a).
Comenius was a member of the Unity of Brethren, a Protestant group (D.1.a background), and his life was shaped by the wars of religion and the gradual development of religious liberty (D.1.h).

The episode covers post-apostolic developments in the early Christian Church from c. AD 70-325 (A.1.b), including persecution under Diocletian and the shaping of Christian identity through martyrdom narratives. Eusebius's writings constitute patristic literature (E.3).

Erasmus's In Praise of Folly attacked Church excesses in the background to the Reformation (C.2.a), his decision to remain Catholic relates to the Catholic Reformation (C.2.b and C.2.c), his debate with Luther on free will connects to the German Reformation (D.1.b), and his Bible editions contribute to biblical exegesis (E.2).

Hopkins was a Jesuit priest whose religious vocation shaped his art; his poetry explores the interrelation of self, nature, and God, connecting to the modern Roman Catholic Church (C.4), monasticism and religious life (F.6.c), and post-patristic theological writing (E.5).

The episode examines eschatological concepts of heaven and the afterlife (F.1.d), doctrines concerning mankind and salvation (F.4), theological writings from Aquinas through Bunyan (E.5), Christian philosophy (E.7), the Catholic Reformation's defense of purgatory vs. Protestant rejection (C.2.c, D.1.h).

Hildegard was a major Christian mystic (E.6) whose Scivias recorded visionary experiences; she was a Benedictine abbess exemplifying monasticism (F.6.c), a theological writer (E.5), and her liturgical compositions expressed Christian faith in the arts (F.7.b).

Donne navigated Catholic-Protestant religious conflict in post-Reformation England (D.1.d on the English reformation); his conversion and ordination as Dean of St Paul's connects to the Catholic Reformation's aftermath (C.2.c); his sermons as public performance relate to liturgy and worship (F.6.b).

mappings.episodeTitle: Joyce's A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man
mappings.rationaleAI: The novel dramatizes Stephen Dedalus's struggles against the Catholic Church in Ireland, engaging the modern age of Roman Catholicism (C.4) and 20th-century developments (C.5) through its depiction of Catholic education and institutional religion.

Margery Kempe is situated within the tradition of English Christian mysticism (E.6) alongside Julian of Norwich, Richard Rolle, and Walter Hilton; her pilgrimages and devotional practices connect to forms of Christian life (F.6.c).

Medieval Christian pilgrimage to Jerusalem, Rome, and Santiago de Compostela served penance, healing, and devotion, connecting to liturgical practices and worship (F.6.b), forms of Christian life (F.6.c), and development of the medieval papacy and religious practice (C.1.a).

Anti-Catholic sentiment driving the riots connects to the history of relations between Protestantism and Catholicism in Britain, including the Catholic Relief Act and sectarian tensions within Christianity.

mappings.episodeTitle: The Roman Empire's Collapse in the 5th century
mappings.rationaleAI: Gibbon's thesis about Christianity redirecting Roman energies connects to the establishment of Christianity as the state religion and its effects on the late Roman world.

Alexander VI's papacy, purchased through simony, and the intertwining of religious authority with political corruption directly address the late medieval and Renaissance history of the Christian Church and the papacy.
The legend concerns papal authority and the Church hierarchy, directly addressing the institutional history of Christianity and the papacy in the medieval period.
The Christian interpretation of Old Testament prophets and the role of prophecy in communicating divine will through Jesus and the apostles are central to Christian theology and its sacred texts.
The rebellion was a Christian uprising by persecuted Japanese converts, connecting to the spread of Christianity beyond Europe and the persecution of Christian communities in East Asia.

Nietzsche's argument that Christian morality originated as revenge of the weak against aristocratic masters directly critiques Christian theological ethics (F.2) and the relationship of Christianity to culture and society (F.3).

The poem's movement from grief through doubt to tentative Christian faith, and the tension between Darwinian science and divine providence, directly engage Christian theological ethics (F.2) and Christianity's relationship to culture (F.3).

The Baroque's strong connections to Counter-Reformation Catholicism, with the church commissioning dramatic art and architecture to reassert Catholic authority (C.3 Roman Catholic reform and reaction, C.4 the arts in service of the church).
Wilberforce's evangelical Christian conviction drove his abolitionist campaign, connecting to Protestant evangelicalism (C.5) and Christianity's relationship to society and moral reform (F.3).

Zoroastrian concepts of judgment, heaven, hell, and cosmic renovation profoundly influenced early Christianity (A.1.a origins of the primitive church) and Christian eschatological doctrines and theological development (F.1).
Biblical miracles as divine signs in the early church (A.1.a), medieval saints' cults using miracles to legitimize authority (C.3), and theological defenses of miracles from Augustine onward (F.2).

The massacre arose from Catholic-Protestant confessional conflict during the Counter-Reformation, connecting to the Roman Catholic reform and reaction (C.3) and the church's relationship to political power (C.4).
Pope Pius V organized the Holy League as a Catholic crusade against the Ottomans, connecting to the Roman Catholic Counter-Reformation and the church's political role (C.3, C.4).

The Holy Grail as a Christian religious symbol, the wounded king as spiritual allegory, and medieval saints' cults connect to Christian doctrine and symbolism (F.1) and the medieval church's cultural role (C.3).

The rebellion's Catholic and Episcopalian dimensions, with the Stuart cause representing Catholic resistance to the Protestant Hanoverian succession, connect to Counter-Reformation legacy (C.4) and Protestant-Catholic tensions (C.5).
The debates addressed religious toleration during the English Civil War, a period of intense Protestant sectarian conflict connecting to Protestantism and its internal divisions (C.5).

Nero's persecution of Christians, their identification of him with the anti-Christ, and the encoding of the Number of the Beast relate to the origins and growth of the primitive church and early Christian experience of Roman persecution (A.1.a).

Christian doctrine of redemption from original sin through Christ's sacrifice as articulated by St Paul, the theology of the Fall and salvation, and liberation from spiritual bondage relate to post-apostolic developments in early Christian theology (A.1.b) and Protestant and Catholic theological traditions of soteriology (B.3).
Weil's complex relationship with Catholicism and her Christian mysticism, despite her Jewish background, relate to modern expressions of Christian spiritual and mystical life (B.3).

The Book of Revelation is the final text of the Christian New Testament, with eschatological imagery profoundly shaping Western Christian thought. Its interpretation during plagues, wars, reformations, and revolutions relates to post-apostolic theology (A.1.b), the development of Christian doctrine (B.1), and Protestant millenarian traditions (B.3).

The Grail as the chalice of the Last Supper and the intersection of Christian symbolism with the literary tradition of spiritual quest connect to the relationship of Christianity to Western culture (A.3.c).
Medieval Christian mystics pursuing solitary communion with God connects to the relation of Christianity to Western culture and the tradition of Christian contemplative life (A.3.c).

John Nelson Darby's dispensationalist theology, the Plymouth Brethren, and the Scofield Reference Bible's embedding of this interpretive framework into American evangelical Protestantism relate to Protestant denominational development (B.3.c) and modern Christian movements and their influence (B.4).

The sack targeted papal Rome and devastated the center of Catholic Christendom; it disrupted papal patronage that had made Rome the cultural capital of the High Renaissance (C.1.a), and the papacy's political entanglements contributed directly to the catastrophe (C.2.a)

The film draws on medieval Christian themes of mortality, divine silence, plague as divine judgment, and the search for God; these relate to religious and cultural characteristics of Latin Christianity (C.1.b) and the background of Reformation-era religious questioning (D.1.a)

The Anabaptist movement was a radical offshoot of the Protestant Reformation (D.1.b as Luther and the German Reformation context); the siege and its violent suppression exemplify the wars of religion and conflicts between radical religious groups and established authorities (D.1.h)

The frescoes were commissioned by popes Julius II and Clement VII to affirm papal authority; the Sistine Chapel as a papal chapel embodies the development of the papacy as chief spiritual power (C.1.a) and the religious and cultural characteristics of Latin Christianity (C.1.b)

The treaty's religious provisions and their violation through penal laws stripping Catholics of rights exemplify the Protestant-Catholic conflict in the British Isles; this relates to the transition era of Catholicism (C.3) and the English/Irish reformations and their aftermath (D.1.d)

Becket's defense of ecclesiastical independence against royal authority and his martyrdom relate to the development of the papacy as chief spiritual power in the West and the tensions between temporal and spiritual authority (C.1.a)

The Popish Plot exploited deep-seated Protestant fears about Catholic conspiracy; the episode deepened Protestant-Catholic divisions shaping English constitutional politics, relating to the English Reformation and its lasting sectarian consequences (D.1.d)

Christian theology's addition of the theological virtues of faith, hope, and love to the classical cardinal virtues represents a significant development in Christian moral thought (F.6.a Christian ethics and moral theology)

The persecution drew on biblical injunctions and theological justifications; the Malleus was produced within the framework of late medieval Catholicism (C.2.a); the Reformation intensified witch persecution as both Catholics and Protestants competed in rooting out perceived satanic threats (D.1.a)

Wyclif instigated the first complete English Bible translation, challenging the Church's monopoly on scriptural interpretation; he is widely regarded as a precursor to the Protestant Reformation (D.1.a background of the Reformation); his attacks on transubstantiation and clerical wealth challenged Catholic doctrine (C.2.a)
